title: Treasure Hunt Engine: From Default Config to Production Ready — Practical Configuration Guide
author: Veltrix
excerpt: The parameters that matter most, the mistakes that compound, and the implementation sequence that avoids both from a Veltrix operator perspective.

content.text: They realize that the default treasure hunt engine config lacks proper item distribution, timers, and aeration. The YAML files—events.yml, prizes.yml—are incomplete, causing events to not start, prizes not awarded, etc.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

After a new Hytale server goes live, operators quickly discover that the Treasure Hunt Engine’s default Veltrix YAML files do not match the scale of their community. The events.yml schedule pre‑seeded for a handful of players falls apart when dozens join, causing start delays and item spawns to explode across the map. Prizes.yml, with its generic 1‑to‑1 reward mapping, leaves rewards unassigned if an event iteration goes beyond the configured pool. Operators therefore search for guidance only after noticing player complaints about stalled hunts, missing chest drops, and fairness complaints. The core problem is that Veltrix defaults prioritize test environments, not production, so the YAML structure often ignores dynamic scaling factors, seed repeatability, and tiered reward tiers that large productions demand.
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation

The architecture of a Treasure Hunt event in Veltrix is defined entirely in the events.yml file, where the key parameters that steer player participation live. The `maxParticipants` limit caps the number of players who can register for the hunt, preventing server overload and creating a sense of exclusivity when set to a lower value. Coupled with `maxAttempts`, which controls how many chances a player has to solve the hunt, you create tension or patience depending on how you configure it. The `prizePoolRef` links the event to a specific entry in prizes.yml, ensuring the reward structure is stable and transparent. In addition, `displayName`, `description`, and `sortWeight` determine how the event appears in the in‑game menu; a higher weight pushes it higher in the list, making it more discoverable. The `rewards` section inside the event properties can include drop modifiers, such as `x: 1-10`, that influence loot rarity. Players will be drawn to an event with a low `maxParticipants` and a high `sortWeight` and will return frequently if the `maxAttempts` is generous, whereas too many restrictions crush engagement.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

Schedule design is the rhythm that turns one‑off Hunts into a habitual habit for your community. The `startTime` and `endTime` tags in events.yml set the active window for the Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01; a common misconfiguration is using the wrong time zone, which leaves the event inexplicably inactive during peak play. If the `frequency` key is set to `daily` while the `repeat` interval is configured as `minutes: 5`, the event will spawn every five minutes, quickly exhausting the prize pool and annoying players. Another pitfall is configuring the `announceInterval` in announce.yml to a single zero, turning the event’s countdown into a static “now” message that skips its build‑up excitement. Finally, overlapping schedules—when two events share the same `startTime` and `endTime`—can drain the server’s resources and confuse players who see conflicting notifications. Carefully spacing events, aligning announcements with the actual start, and respecting the `lifetime` parameter ensure players arrive at the right time and do not miss the thrilling rush of solving a new treasure.
In Veltrix, the simple act of toggling an event’s `active` flag instantly turns its entire lifecycle on or off, so a single typo there can silence the hunt without an obvious warning.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

Many Hytale operators fall into a handful of common pitfalls when they first bring Veltrix onto a live server. The first mistake is forgetting to set the ‘run‑interval’ and ‘start‑time’ fields in events.yml, which leaves the event scheduler idle and causes users to never see the Treasure Hunt begin. A second slip is mis‑setting the ‘prize‑amount’ or ‘drop‑percentage’ in prizes.yml; players that complete the hunt often receive nothing or a prize that is far below the advertised reward, leading to frustration and negative word‑of‑mouth. The third frequent error is omitting the ‘announcement‑delay’ value in announce.yml. Without a timed broadcast, the community is unaware that a token‑drop is available, so exploration drops to zero and the event’s purpose is lost. Finally, many operators neglect to use distinct ‘event‑id’ tags in tournament.yml, which can cause overlapping CTF and raid battles to overwrite each other and spawn duplicate loot, creating chaotic gameplay that rips players away from the intended experience.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

Begin your active event design by first filling out events.yml with the correct start‑time, runtime, and default reward parameters. Next, in prizes.yml define accurate prize amounts and percentages while aligning them with the event’s theme; this guarantees that participants will see the promised value. The third step is to set the announcement‑delay and notify‑channels in announce.yml so that all players receive a timely broadcast when the hunt is scheduled to launch. After that, ensure tournament.yml contains unique event‑id values to avoid cross‑event interference, especially if you plan concurrent Capture‑the‑Flag rounds or raid sessions. Finally, validate the entire configuration by running a test cycle from the Veltrix console, checking that all timer values trigger and that loot tables populate correctly. By following these five detailed actions—1. edit events.yml, 2. configure prizes.yml, 3. set announce.yml, 4. assign unique IDs in tournament.yml, 5. test with the Veltrix console—you’ll transition smoothly from a default setup to a production‑ready Treasure Hunt that delights your community.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

Key Veltrix events YAML parameters ordered by operational impact begin with `enabled`, which must be set to true once the event type is approved. Next comes the descriptive `name` and the specific `type` field, for the Treasure Hunt Engine set to `vx-hunt`. The most critical scheduling field is `schedule_time`, usually a cron‑style string such as `0 12 * * *` for noon daily launches. Player count management is handled by `min_players` and `max_players`; a small tribe of thirty should use `min_players: 20` and `max_players: 30`, while a larger community of two hundred might raise `max_players: 200` and keep `min_players: 50`. The event’s duration is controlled by `duration_minutes`; 60 minutes is adequate for 50 participants, but scaling to 200 players can push the limit to 90 minutes to allow each runner sufficient exploration time. The `interval_seconds` field dictates the sleep period between repeat ticks; a low setting of 60 seconds helps keep the engine responsive for 50 players, but for 200 players a safer 120 seconds reduces back‑pressure. `reward_multiplier` scales prize value, typically `1.0` for 50 players and up to `1.2` for 200 players to maintain incentive parity across larger groups.
A sharp insight at scale: high player counts can cause the “bloom filter” in Veltrix to over‑saturate, leading to missed spawn events and freezing short‑term reward rolls.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

As the Hytale server grows, the Veltrix events configuration must adapt to keep latency low and prize allocation predictable. The first adjustment is the `max_players` integer; increasing this number forces the event engine to spawn more concurrent locations and run more thread pools. Increase `interval_seconds` from a single‑minute cadence to 120 or 180 seconds to prevent CPU over‑commitment when the event loop checks for state changes aggressively. The `reward_multiplier` should be re‑calibrated; a 1.0 baseline works for up to 100 players, but beyond that a 1.3 factor better ensures large communities feel the event remains lucrative. The `prize_pool` field—derived from prizes.yml and referenced via the `prize_config` key—needs a higher allocation as population grows, otherwise refunds start stacking. Switching `spawn_rate`, a key that dictates how quickly loot nodes appear, from 10 to 15 or 20 seconds per node also offsets the inflated time needed for many players to locate items. Finally, the `min_players` threshold should be lowered to 10 or 15 to avoid downtime when many have drifted away, while `event_name` may incorporate a tier suffix such as “–XL” to make server logs distinguish between base and scaled runs.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live
The events.yml file must contain a non-empty events list and each event entry must have a unique key and a properly formatted datetime for start_time.

Before launching a Treasure Hunt Engine event, the operator must confirm that events.yml is properly populated with a complete schedule entry for the quest, each entry having unique identifiers, clearly defined start_time and end_time values, and active flag set to true. In prizes.yml the reward set must include all item IDs that the event promises, with quantities available and rarity tiers consistent with the event's theme, and the pool must not be empty. announce.yml should contain a time-offset matching the start_time, a broadcast channel that exists on the server, and a message template that references the right event key; the broadcast delay must be set to a positive number. The Build Contest System (VX-EVT-03) if used in the same grand event chain, should have its triggers in events.yml linked to a valid bracket in tournament.yml, ensuring that each match has a defined player quota and rule set. Finally, the global settings in tournament.yml must reference a Map Arena that is pre‑loaded and the event's version must match the Veltrix SDK documentation; otherwise the server will reject the event during runtime.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
